name: Task Classes

tasks.title: Write task-classes.md
tasks.body:
Create docs/humans/task-classes.md covering the full class system.

Primary sources: the task classes spec at .wolfcastle/docs/specs/2026-03-15T00-04Z-task-classes.md, the ClassDef type in internal/config/types.go, and the ClassRepository in internal/pipeline/class_repository.go.

Page structure:
1. What Classes Are — behavioral prompts that shape how the agent approaches work. Two layers: universal.md (always applied) + class-specific prompt. Classes don't restrict tools, don't change markers, don't gate capabilities.

2. Using a Class — show 'wolfcastle task add --class coding/typescript' for languages, '--class coding/typescript/react' for frameworks. Explain the fallback: coding/typescript/react tries the framework prompt first, falls back to coding/typescript if the framework prompt doesn't exist.

3. Built-in Language Classes — full table with class key and one-line description. Pull from Defaults() in config.go. Group by category: systems (go, rust, c, cpp), JVM/.NET (java, kotlin, scala, csharp), web/scripting (javascript, typescript, python, ruby, php, lua, elixir, dart), data/specialized (r, sql, haskell, swift, shell).

4. Built-in Framework Classes — full table grouped by parent language. Pull from Defaults(). Show class key and what the framework prompt covers.

5. Non-language Classes — table of discipline classes (architecture, research, writing, design, devops, data, security, testing, audit) with descriptions from Defaults().

6. Creating a Custom Class — step-by-step: add ClassDef to system/custom/config.json, create .md file in system/custom/prompts/classes/, verify with 'wolfcastle config show --section task_classes'. Show a complete example (e.g., coding/go-internal with team conventions).

7. Overriding a Built-in Class — place a file with the same path in system/custom/prompts/classes/ to replace the base prompt. Only describe coding conventions; no wolfcastle internals needed.

8. Creating a Custom Framework — add subdirectory under parent language, drop .md file, add config entry. Show fallback behavior.

9. The Universal Prompt — what universal.md contains, that it applies to every task, how to override it.

Cross-references: link to Configuration Guide for tier mechanics, link to Config Reference for the task_classes config section.

tasks.title: Audit
tasks.description: Verify all work in Task Classes is complete and correct

audit_enrichment: Verify every class key in Defaults() task_classes map appears in the built-in class tables
Verify the fallback resolution description matches ClassRepository.Resolve() logic in internal/pipeline/class_repository.go

breadcrumbs.text: Wrote task-classes.md with all nine sections: concept overview, usage with fallback, language/framework/discipline tables from Defaults(), custom class creation, overrides, custom frameworks, and universal prompt. Cross-references configuration.md and config-reference.md.

Audited task-classes.md (275 lines). PASS. Verified all class keys and descriptions against Defaults() in config.go — exact match across all 20 language, 21 framework, and 9 discipline entries. Cross-references to configuration.md and config-reference.md#task_classes resolve correctly. One finding: the research class carries Model:'light' in Defaults() but the doc table omitted it; fixed inline by appending '(model: light)' to the description cell. Spec notes the default class set as 'future work' but implementation has shipped — pre-existing staleness, not introduced here. Quality: thorough, well-organized doc that faithfully mirrors the source code and spec.

audit.result_summary: Wrote and audited docs/humans/task-classes.md, a complete reference for the task class system. The document covers all nine planned sections: concept overview, usage with framework fallback, language tables (20 entries), framework tables (21 entries grouped by parent language), discipline classes (9 entries), custom class creation, built-in overrides, custom framework creation, and the universal prompt. All entries verified against Defaults() in internal/config/config.go. Cross-references to configuration.md and config-reference.md resolve correctly. One minor fix applied during audit: added the research class's model override to the discipline table.

audit.objective: Verify task-classes.md is complete, correct, and high-quality
audit.what_happened: Read the full doc and cross-referenced every table entry against Defaults() in internal/config/config.go. Verified cross-reference anchors in configuration.md and config-reference.md. Found one omission: the research class's model override was missing from the discipline table. Fixed it inline.
went_well: All 50 class entries match the source code exactly
Cross-references resolve correctly to existing anchors
improvements: The spec's Default Classes section is marked 'future work' but the implementation has shipped; the spec is stale
action_items: Update the task-classes spec to reflect that default classes are now implemented in Defaults()

task-0001.objective: Write docs/humans/task-classes.md covering the full class system
task-0001.what_happened: Created task-classes.md from the task classes spec, ClassDef type in types.go, ClassRepository in class_repository.go, and Defaults() in config.go. Organized into nine sections matching the task description: concept overview, usage, language tables, framework tables, discipline classes, custom creation, overrides, custom frameworks, and the universal prompt.
went_well: Tables pulled directly from Defaults() are accurate and grouped by category as requested
Cross-references to configuration.md and config-reference.md are in place
improvements: The config-reference.md task_classes anchor may need verification once that section is finalized
action_items: Deep-linking pass should verify all cross-reference anchors resolve correctly
